[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: [ ]’s new face EXPLAINED!!! by Stella Tago — In The Mood Magazine
[METADATA/INTRO]: That face on the other side… do you know her? Have you seen her face pull down to the sink? Do you reach your hands up to your temples, just behind the hairline, caress the pressure points, and pull back...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: That face on the other side… do you know her? 
 Have you seen her face pull down to the sink? Do you reach your hands up to your temples, just behind the hairline, caress the pressure points, and pull back ever so slightly? Nothing too noticeable, just enough to turn the brightness up one degree. 
 Just enough to feel a little tension. 
 Just a little… ah! [Ahhhh…] 
 HEAD IN A VICE 
 An early scene in Terry Gilliam’s dystopian satire Brazil (1985) shows the protagonist’s elderly aristocrat mother admonishing her child in the midst of a facelift consultation. Lamenting the protagonist’s unwillingness to ascend the arbitrary ranks of state bureaucracy, his mother barely flinches while a plastic surgeon gleefully ratchets his hands to the side of her face and pulls her cheeks back as far as they can go, so that the bare skeleton, the nerves and vessels and muscle tissue, are all made visible. The tension of the skin barely stops the contents of her whole head from falling out.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2010-04-22
[TITLE]: facelift | Drink My Words
[METADATA/INTRO]: ON THE EVE OF MY FORTIETH BIRTHDAY, I found myself in a Midwest hotel with a bottle of Hendrick’s gin. True, the hotel boasted a state-of-the-art fitness center, and my room had a fridge, but I was in the middle...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: I used to insist I would age gracefully, that the phrase “plastic surgery” would never escape my lips, which would never be injected with collagen. During the height of my teenage soap opera addiction, I watched a character from The Young and the Restless, Katherine Chancellor, receive an on-air facelift. A written warning flashed across the screen, followed by scalpels, bloody flesh, heavy bandages and later, bruising. I was mortified that a beautiful woman would elect to have someone loosen the skin on her face with a knife and then yank it up like a pair of knee socks. 
 As I get older, procedures like laser skin resurfacing and facelifts no longer bring to my mind torture methods from old school horror movies. Rather, these treatments fall into the suspense or adventure comedy genre, like when I recently found myself engrossed in an older friend’s story about flying to Costa Rica for a bargain facelift. I was on the edge of my seat, asking, “And then what happened?”

[TITLE]: Architects of Absurdity: Navigating Gilliam's Beautiful Nightmar…
[METADATA/INTRO]: Architects of Absurdity: Navigating Gilliam's Beautiful Nightmares In this holiday edition of Look Behind the Look, we unwrap the layers of Terry Gilliam's 1985 film Brazil, a retro futurist fever dream that cleverly disguises its incisive critique of bureaucracy and...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Architects of Absurdity: Navigating Gilliam's Beautiful Nightmares 
 In this holiday edition of Look Behind the Look, we unwrap the layers of Terry Gilliam's 1985 film Brazil, a retro futurist fever dream that cleverly disguises its incisive critique of bureaucracy and authoritarianism as a Christmas movie. Join us as we explore the film's rich aesthetics, from its elaborate production design to the campy hair and makeup, revealing how these visuals reflect the emotional machinery of a society struggling under the weight of conformity and denial. Discover how Brazil remains relevant today, echoing our contemporary challenges with digital systems and the normalization of crisis.
